coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photodegradation of perfluorocarboxylic acids (PFCAs) using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped TiO2 photocatalysts: A comparative study between exfoliated graphite and mussel shell-derived CQDs

coredata.dc:description: Remediation of perfluorocarboxylic acids (PFCAs) is crucial due to their widespread distribution, persistence, and harmful effects on aquatic life, especially as short-chain fluorinated compounds are increasingly used as substitutes after the global ban on long-chain PFCAs. This pioneering study explores the photodegradation of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA-C8) and several short-chain PFCAs using carbon quantum dot (CQD)-doped TiO₂. We synthesized CQDs via two innovative methods: electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rods (Exfol.CQD) and a hydrothermal process using mussel shell biomass (MCQD). The structural and morphological properties of CQDs, TiO₂, and CQD/TiO₂ composites were characterized. Photodegradation tests showed that Exfol.CQD/TiO₂ significantly improved PFOA degradation under visible light, achieving a twofold increase compared to pure TiO₂. Degradation rates for C3-C6 short-chain PFCAs with Exfol.CQD/TiO₂ ranged from 22 % to 40 %. PFOA decomposed stepwise into shorter-chain PFCAs and F⁻ ions, maintaining excellent reusability after three cycles. In contrast, the MCQD/TiO₂ photocatalyst needed further optimization due to decreased performance. Our research has shown that using pure water and ethanol as electrolytes facilitates the synthesis of high-purity CQDs without the need for chemical additives. This study sheds light on the previously underexplored area of photodegradation of PFCAs via CQD/TiO₂ composites, highlighting their potential applications in wastewater treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Remediation of perfluorocarboxylic acids (PFCAs) is crucial due to their widespread distribution, persistence, and harmful effects on aquatic life, especially as short-chain fluorinated compounds are increasingly used as substitutes after the global ban on long-chain PFCAs. This pioneering study explores the photodegradation of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA-C8) and several short-chain PFCAs using carbon quantum dot (CQD)-doped TiO₂. We synthesized CQDs via two innovative methods: electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rods (Exfol.CQD) and a hydrothermal process using mussel shell biomass (MCQD). The structural and morphological properties of CQDs, TiO₂, and CQD/TiO₂ composites were characterized. Photodegradation tests showed that Exfol.CQD/TiO₂ significantly improved PFOA degradation under visible light, achieving a twofold increase compared to pure TiO₂. Degradation rates for C3-C6 short-chain PFCAs with Exfol.CQD/TiO₂ ranged from 22 % to 40 %. PFOA decomposed stepwise into shorter-chain PFCAs and F⁻ ions, maintaining excellent reusability after three cycles. In contrast, the MCQD/TiO₂ photocatalyst needed further optimization due to decreased performance. Our research has shown that using pure water and ethanol as electrolytes facilitates the synthesis of high-purity CQDs without the need for chemical additives. This study sheds light on the previously underexplored area of photodegradation of PFCAs via CQD/TiO₂ composites, highlighting their potential applications in wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the 1940s, per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) have been widely used in various industries owing to their exceptional physicochemical properties, including robust carbon-fluorine bonds, stability, fire and weather resistance, non-wettability, and hydrophobicity. The application of these substances encompasses different industries, including textiles, where they are utilized as coating materials, surfactants, and fire retardants. In recent years, the removal of PFAS has emerged as a significant concern due to their harmful effects on human health and the environment, particularly in aquatic ecosystems [1,2].

Perfluorocarboxylic acids (PFCAs) represent a subset of PFAS characterized by a carboxylic group at the terminal end. This structural feature is present in a chain of perfluorinated carbon atoms of varying lengths. Perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), a persistent member of the PFCA family, has attracted significant attention due to its wide distribution in aquatic environments, biota, and humans, as well as its adverse impacts on both human health and ecosystems [3]. Despite regulatory surveillance by organizations such as the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) and the European Union (EU), which seek to restrict the production and usage of PFOA, there has been a rising trend in the detection of PFOA in water environments [4]. The remarkable chemical stability of PFOA, attributed primarily to its strong carbon-fluorine bonds, poses a formidable challenge for environmental remediation efforts [5]. Its bioaccumulative nature, toxicity, and potential carcinogenicity in humans and wildlife highlight the urgent need for effective and economical methods to remove it from contaminated sites, water, and wastewater streams. On the other hand, the inclusion of long-chain PFCAs (C>7) in the list of Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) has resulted in the cessation of their production by the manufacturing sector. This has been supplanted by the use of short-chain PFCAs (C≤7) and alternative fluorinated compounds. However, it is important to note that the solubility of short-chain PFCAs increases as the number of carbon atoms decreases, resulting in greater mobility and a higher likelihood of groundwater and soil contamination. Furthermore, the fluorinated components of these alternatives have the potential to generate persistent and hazardous transformation byproducts. Therefore, it is critical to address the degradation or breakdown of PFCA pollutants in wastewater [6–8]. Different methods have been proposed for treating PFCAs, including adsorption by carbon-based sorbents [9], ultrasound [10], membrane filtration [2], chemical oxidation and reduction [11], photocatalytic degradation [12], electrochemical oxidation [1], and incineration [13]. Among these methods, photocatalytic degradation is a highly promising method due to its eco-friendly nature and utilization of light as a sustainable and cost-effective source of energy. This method can effectively degrade pollutants into less harmful byproducts without triggering secondary pollution, making it an appealing environmental remediation choice [7,14].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess unique properties that make them attractive for implementation in photocatalytic processes. Their ability to convert long wavelengths of solar energy into potent chemical energy, with their widespread availability, cost-effectiveness, high solubility, and low toxicity, position them as ideal candidates for advancing a new generation of carbon-based nanomaterials [15–17]. For example, hydrothermal synthesis of CQD and nitrogen-doped CQD (NCQD) as photocatalysts utilizing Pomelo juice have been carried out to investigate the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) under white light and sunlight conditions [18]. The results demonstrated that both CQDs served as excellent photocatalysts for the degradation of MB under sunlight irradiation. However, poor electron transfer inside CQDs has restricted their application [16]. CQDs can be coupled with semiconductors like titanium dioxide (TiO2) to overcome this and enhance photocatalytic activities [19]. TiO2 is one of the most studied photocatalysts in wastewater treatment due to its photocatalytic activity under UV light, chemical stability, non-toxicity, and low cost [20,21]. However, its photocatalytic efficiency is limited because of the low harvest of visible light due to its large energy band gap (Eg = 3.2 Ev) and fast recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers [15,20]. CQDs may enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 by using visible and near-infrared spectra of sunlight via bandgap engineering and restraining the recombination of electron-hole pairs [15,22]. Several studies highlight the superior photocatalytic performance of CQD/TiO2 composite photocatalysts in degrading various pollutants, indicating their potential for applications in wastewater treatment [23,24]. For example, NCQD-decorated TiO2 composites and pure TiO2 were studied for the degradation of MB under UV radiation, and the addition of NCQD enhanced the degradation of MB by 93 % within 60 minutes [15]. Similarly, Liu et al. reported the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) using a one-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently, the composite material of NSCQDs combined with zinc oxide (ZnO) was fabricated through a room-temperature impregnation technique. The photocatalytic analysis demonstrated that the degradation rate of rhodamine blue (RhB) by the NSCQDs/ZnO composite reached an impressive 91.5 %, a significant improvement compared to the degradation rate of ZnO alone (49 %) [25]. Silva et al. also employed the hydrothermal method to synthesize various CQD/TiO2 photocatalysts. Their investigation focused on the degradation of oxalinic acid (OXA) under solar light irradiation in different environments. The results indicated that significant reductions in OXA half-life time (t1/2) were observed for composites utilizing CQDs synthesized from citric acid, demonstrating decreases of 91 %, 79 %, and 85 % in the respective environments. Furthermore, the photodegradation of various model pollutants under visible light was improved by incorporating CQDs into TiO2 sheets using the hydrothermal method [26]. In the literature, various studies have explored the photodegradation of PFOA using TiO2-doped photocatalytic composites incorporating Ag [27], Cu [12], Pb [28], Pt [29], BiOBr [5] nanoparticles (NPs), multi-walled carbon nanotubes [30], graphene oxide [4], and reduced graphene oxides [31]. Across these studies, the photodegradation efficiency has consistently shown enhancement compared to pure TiO2. Chen et al. [5] investigated the photodegradation of PFOA using BiOBr/TiO2 photocatalysts under irradiation with a 300 W Xe lamp (320–780 nm), achieving a 99.5 % degradation efficiency within 100 minutes. In another study, Chen et al. [28] reported a 99.9 % photodegradation efficiency of PFOA using a Pb/TiO2 photocatalyst composite. This was accomplished under UV light (254 nm) with a 400 W medium-pressure mercury lamp over 12 hours. Notably, these studies achieved high PFOA degradation efficiencies utilizing high-power xenon or mercury lamps.

Here in this study, LED lights were employed, which offer several advantages, including lower cost, higher energy efficiency, longer lifespan, and reduced environmental impact compared to xenon and mercury lamps [32]. Moreover, this research introduces, for the first time, biomass-derived CQDs and graphitic carbon source CQDs doped TiO2 photocatalysts for the degradation of PFOA and, particularly, short-chain PFCAs. These CQD-based materials provide significant environmental benefits over metal-based photocatalysts, which may pose toxicity and environmental contamination risks. Our approach aligns with the principles of green chemistry, addressing the growing demand for sustainable solutions in environmental remediation.

Seashell waste, amounting to more than 17 million tons annually globally, presents a significant environmental challenge, primarily being disposed of in the ocean or landfills. For this research, mussels, known for their substantial shell content, were selected as the natural carbon source for producing CQDs, demonstrating a sustainable approach [33,34]. Utilizing biomass as a precursor for the synthesis of CQDs is both environmentally sustainable and cost-effective, in accordance with the principles of green chemistry. Several studies in the literature have investigated the use of CQDs to create CQDs/TiO2 composites for the degradation of various organic pollutants. However, most of these studies have utilized carbon sources derived from non-biomass sources, such as citric acid [35,36] or glycerol [37]. The hydrothermal method allows for the production of CQDs with controlled sizes and customized surface functionalities. Genç et al. developed CQDs through a green synthesis method utilizing Ginkgo biloba and employed microwave irradiation and achieve particle sizes ranging from 15 to 20 nanometers, which exceeds the optimal CQD size of 3–10 nanometers [38]. Meanwhile, in the present study, CQDs were synthesized using the electrochemical exfoliation method by using graphite rods as a carbon source. The electrochemical exfoliation method for synthesizing CQDs from graphite rods has emerged as a highly promising technique due to its simplicity, scalability, and environmental friendliness [39]. This process leads to the breakdown of graphite rods by rotating electrolytes, leading to the formation of CQDs. Unlike traditional top-down methods that require strong acids and harsh conditions, this approach uses common apparatus at room temperature and avoids hazardous chemicals [40]. The properties of CQDs in this method are significantly influenced by the applied voltage and the composition of the electrolyte [55]. Our study has demonstrated that utilizing pure water and ethanol as electrolytes can produce high-purity CQDs without additional chemical additives. When comparing established methods such as arc discharge and laser ablation, it is evident that these techniques are recognized for their capacity to produce CQDs. However, they are often associated with significant energy requirements and the need for complex apparatus. In contrast, our methods present a more sustainable and cost-effective alternative, particularly when utilizing biomass and simplified electrochemical configurations. Regarding chemical vapor deposition (CVD) and mechanical exfoliation, although these techniques are commonly employed for the synthesis of graphene, they are less suitable for CQDs due to their inherent complexity and associated costs [41]. Our hydrothermal and electrochemical methodologies provide a more straightforward and scalable approach for CQD synthesis. Microwave-assisted and ultrasonic synthesis methods are also utilized for CQD production; however, they may face challenges related to the control of particle size and surface functionalities. In comparison, our methodologies offer enhanced control over these parameters, particularly through the hydrothermal process that leverages biomass resources. This study addresses the unexplored area of photodegradation of PFCAs with different chain lengths using CQD/TiO2 composites under visible and UVC light irradiations in wastewater treatment.

The structural characterization results confirm the successful synthesis of pure CQDs using both methods. They also verify the formation of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 photocatalytic composites with enhanced optical properties. These properties were analyzed using Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-Vis), photoluminescence (PL), UV-Vis Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (UV-Vis-DRS), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Additionally, intensity-modulated photovoltage/photocurrent spectroscopy (IMVS/IMPS) and photocurrent responses were used in the analysis. During the degradation process, PFCAs undergo sequential breakdown into intermediate shorter-chain PFCAs, with defluorination occurring progressively as the reaction advances, ultimately leading to the breakdown of these persistent compounds. Reusability assessments were also conducted on both photocatalysts following photodegradation without chemical regeneration.

Titanium (IV) butoxide, NaOH, NaCl, acetic acid, ethanol, methanol, hydrochloric acid, ultra-high pure water for chromatography (absolute for analysis), and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA-C8), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA-C6), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA-C5), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA-C4), and perfluoropropanoic acid (PFPrA-C3) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Ultra-pure water was accessed via the Ege University’s central water distillation source. Mussel shells were provided by the food market. Graphite rods as carbon sources for the electrochemical exfoliation method were also obtained. All chemicals were utilized without further purification.

CQDs were synthesized through hydrothermal and electrochemical exfoliation methods, employing biomass-derived mussel shells and graphite rods as carbon sources, respectively. The synthesis of CQDs through the electrochemical exfoliation method was carried out using a two-electrode setup. Two graphite rods (10 cm in length and 0.6 cm in diameter) served as working electrode and counter electrode which were rinsed with ethanol and DI water via ultrasonic treatment for 30 minutes. The electrolyte solution was a mixture of 0.1 M NaOH and 0.1 M NaCl in ethanol/water (70:30 v/v). The graphite rods were immersed in the salt solution, positioned parallel to each other with a 1.2 cm gap, and extending 8 cm in length. A constant voltage (5 V) was applied to the electrodes via a DC power supply with continuous stirring to ensure complete agitation during exfoliation. The anode graphite rod corroded upon initiating voltage and gradually turned yellow within an hour. Over time, the color intensified, eventually evolving to a red shade. The solution was then subjected to centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 30 min. Following centrifugation, the solution underwent filtration through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to isolate Exfol.CQDs. Next, a purification process was performed using a dialysis bag with a 1000 MW cutoff for 24 hours. The process is depicted in visual detail in Fig. S1 (a).

Mussel shells were cleaned and washed with ultra-pure water to remove all impurities. They were subjected to overnight treatment with 0.2 M hydrochloric acid solutions to ensure enhanced purification. Afterward, mussel shells were dried in the oven for 2 hours until complete drying. Finally, the dried samples were manually reduced and blended into powder form, which was then sifted through a 150 µm sieve. Mussel shell-derived CQDs (MCQDs) were prepared using a hydrothermal method. Initially, 2 g of biomass powder was mixed with 40 ml of DI water. This mixture underwent 10 minutes of ultrasonication, followed by 15 minutes of magnetic stirring before being transferred to a Teflon/PPL-lined stainless-steel autoclave for hydrothermal reaction. After a hydrothermal reaction at 200°C for 480 min, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and the same isolation and purification processes were employed in obtaining Exfol.CQDs were carried out. A visual representation of the MCQD synthesis process is provided in Fig. S1 (b). The resulting MCQD and Exfol.CQD solutions were frozen and then lyophilized to obtain powdered CQDs.

TiO2-MCQD, TiO2-Exfol.CQD and pure TiO2 photocatalysts were prepared using titanium butoxide through the sol-gel method. The sol-gel synthesis followed the procedure outlined by Dönmez et al. [42]. In brief, 10 ml of titanium butoxide was introduced into 20 ml of ethanol with vigorous stirring. Subsequently, 2 ml of MCQDs (2 mg/ml) was added to the solution. This homogeneous mixture was then slowly added dropwise into another mixture composed of 40 ml of ethanol, 1 ml of DI water, and 4 ml of acetic acid under vigorous stirring. The colloidal suspension was stirred for 6 hours and left for an aging process for 60 hours to form a gel structure. The synthesized catalyst was dried at 80 °C for 48 hours and then calcinated at 500 °C for 3 hours. TiO2 was prepared using the same procedure but without adding CQDs solution, instead including 2 ml of DI water.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was conducted using the PerkinElmer Spectrum100 instrument, spanning the infrared range of 650–4000 cm−1. The high-resolution transmission mode at 4 cm−1 was employed for detailed characterization. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was collected using Panalytical Empyrean in 2θ range of 0–80 ֠ with 2 degrees. min−1 scan rate. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha using 100–4000 eV monochromatic Al Kɑ radiation. UV-Vis was conducted on GENESIS 10S UV-Vis (Thermo Scientific) spectrometer in the spectral range between 200 and 400 nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken from Hitachi HT7800 electron microscope at a 100 kV accelerating voltage in high-resolution mode. Aberration Corrected High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (AC-HR-TEM) images were collected on an FEI Titan3 G2 equipped with double Cs-correctors at 300 kV. All TEM and HRTEM specimens were prepared using the standard drop-casting method on lacey carbon copper TEM grids. PL spectra were recorded by the Edinburgh FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature. The UV-Vis-DRS of the powdered samples was measured using a Shimadzu 2600 spectrophotometer equipped with an ISR attachment. This analysis was performed to evaluate the optical properties and determine the bandgap energies of the photocatalysts. EIS was performed using Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (AUT 204 S) with a frequency response analyzer (FRA) module through a frequency range of 10−3 Hz to 106 Hz. Data from EIS were obtained as Nyquist plots and fitted to an equivalent electrical circuit using Autolab impedance analysis software (Nova 2.1). In a conventional three-electrode system, a modified FTO electrode, Ag/AgCl, and platinum wire were employed as the working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. Chronoamperometry measurements for photocurrent response analysis were carried out under intermittent light with 470 nm wavelength irradiation at 50-second intervals. Photocurrent responses and IMVS/IMPS measurements were conducted using an electrochemical workstation that consisted of a Metrohm Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat equipped with an FRA32M module and an Autolab Optical Bench kit. A 470 nm (blue) LED light was used as the light source, and the AC amplitude was set to 10 % of the light intensity value. IMVS/IMPS measurements were performed between 20 kHz and 100 mHz, with ten data points per decade.

The concentration of PFCAs and intermediates was quantified using liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS, Shimadzu LCMS-8040) operating in negative electrospray ionization (ESI−) mode. Chromatographic separation was performed on an XDB-C18 column (2.1 mm × 150 mm, 5 µm) with a flow rate of 300 µL/min and an injection volume of 10 μL. The mobile phase consisted of two solvents: solvent A, comprising 20 mM ammonium acetate in 5 % methanol and 95 % ultrapure water, and solvent B, consisting of 100 % methanol. Initially, the mobile phase composition was set at 90 % (A) and 10 % (B) and maintained for 3 minutes. Following this, a gradient elution profile was employed with solvent B varying as follows: from 3 to 6.7 minutes, held at 100 % B, and from 6.7 to 9 minutes, a linear decrease from 100 % to 10 % B. Throughout the analysis, the electrospray ionization capillary voltage was set at −3.5 kV, with desolvation and heat block temperatures maintained at 250°C and 400°C, respectively.

The fluoride ion (F⁻) concentration was determined using an ion chromatograph (IC) (Thermo Scientific Dionex ICS-5000 DC) equipped with an autosampler and a Dionex IonPac AS19 analytical column (4 × 250 mm). The mobile phase consisted of 12 mM and 45 mM KOH prepared in ultrapure water, with a flow rate maintained at 1 ml/min. The gradient elution profile was as follows: 12 mM KOH from 0 to 10 minutes, followed by a transition to 45 mM KOH from 10 to 30 minutes, and finally reverting to 12 mM KOH for 0.1 minutes. Defluorination ratios were calculated using Eq. (1):

The photocatalytic degradation of PFOA-C8 was investigated using pure TiO2, MCQD/TiO2, and Exfol.CQD/TiO2 composites. Additionally, the photodegradation of short-chain PFCAs (C3 to C6) was studied using the Exfol.CQD/TiO2 photocatalyst. The experiments were conducted in a 150 ml cylindrical quartz reactor containing 100 ml of either a PFOA solution or a mixed short-chain PFCA solution. Each component of the solution was maintained at a concentration of 100 µg/L, and the photocatalyst concentration was set at 0.1 g/L. The initial pH of the solution was adjusted to 5 using a 0.1 mM HCl solution to ensure consistent reaction conditions.

Prior to light irradiation, the reactor was stirred in the dark for 30 minutes to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and the target compounds. Subsequently, the solution was exposed to either four UVC lamps (16 W total, emitting in the 100–280 nm range) or a visible light source comprising 32 lamps (200 W total, emitting in the 400–700 nm range). The reactor setup for UVC and visible light irradiation is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1(a)-(b). Samples were withdrawn at regular time intervals during the photodegradation process for analysis.

Fig. 2(a) presents the FTIR results for MCQD, Exfol.CQD, TiO2, MCQD/TiO2, and Exfol.CQD/TiO2. In the FTIR spectra of Exfol.CQD, a prominent transmittance peak at 3287 cm−1, is attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl (O-H) and amino (N-H) groups. Additionally, peaks at 2974 cm−1, 2932 cm−1, and 2875 cm−1 arise from the stretching vibration of C-H bonds. These C-H bonds potentially originate from methyl and methylene groups associated with aliphatic hydrocarbons [43]. Peaks at 1671 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO and CC bonds, respectively [44]. Furthermore, transmittance peaks of C-O bonds were observed at 1070 cm−1 [45]. In the case of MCQD, a broad peak at 3247 cm−1 is attributed to the symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibrations of -O-H or -N-H bonds, indicating the presence of carboxyl and amino groups [46,47]. A weaker peak at 2930 cm−1 corresponds to C-H bond vibrations [44,48]. The stretching vibrations of CC and CO bonds yield distinct peaks at 1570 cm−1 and within the range of 1633–1698 cm−1, respectively [46,49,50]. Additionally, the peak at 1400 cm−1 is associated with the vibration of C-N bonds on the MCQD surface [1,12,13]. In contrast, the peak at 1245 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C-O-C bonds on the MCQD surface [51]. Peaks at 1075 cm−1 and 1040 cm−1 result from C-O and C-O-C stretching bonds [44,52]. Weaker peaks within the 675–900 cm−1 range indicate the bending vibration of the C-H group [45,50].

Overall, both Exfol.CQD and MCQD show strong O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, suggesting they both contain significant amounts of hydroxyl and amino groups. The presence of C-H, CO, and CC bonds in both materials indicates the presence of aliphatic and aromatic hydrocarbons and carbonyl groups. MCQDs exhibit additional peaks indicating C-N and C-O-C bonds, suggesting a more complex surface chemistry with possible amine and ether groups. Overall, the FTIR analysis confirms the presence of various functional groups on the surfaces of MCQD and Exfol.CQD, which are crucial for their interaction with TiO2 and their performance in photocatalytic applications. This detailed understanding of the surface chemistry helps explain the enhanced photocatalytic properties observed in the composites.

In FTIR spectra of TiO2, no O-H vibration bond is detected in the FTIR spectrum due to the complete water content evaporation at 600°C of calcination. Nevertheless, transmittance below 882 cm−1 still indicates the Ti-O-Ti vibration bonds [24,53]. The MCQD/TiO2 and Exfol.CQD/ TiO2 spectrum showed broader peaks around 3348 cm−1 and 1638 cm−1 due to O-H bond stretching and bending vibrations. The appearance of new red-shifted transmittance peaks at approximately 1552 cm−1, 1441 cm−1, and 1345 cm−1 is attributed to the CC, C-N, and C-O-C functional groups within CQDs, respectively. Furthermore, a redshift in the peak below 882 cm−1 indicates a combination of Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C vibrations, suggesting the successful integration of CQDs and TiO2, as well as the transfer of photoinduced electrons [53,54]. In the spectra of Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD/TiO2, the absence of characteristic peaks attributable to the low concentration of CQDs is notable. However, the widening of the peak below 880 cm−1 and the consequent red shift observed in these composites suggest the presence of Exfol.CQD and MCQD within the TiO2 structure strongly indicating the formation of Ti-O-C bonds [55,56].

The XRD patterns depicted in Fig. 2(b) illustrate the phase structure of pristine TiO2, Exfol.CQD/TiO2, and MCQD/TiO2 composites. The results indicate a close resemblance between the XRD patterns of the composites and those of TiO2. In TiO2, characteristic anatase phase peaks are evident at 25.3⁰ (101), 37.9⁰ (004), 48.1⁰ (200), 54⁰ (105), 55.1⁰ (211), 62.7⁰ (204), 68.9⁰ (116), 70.3⁰ (220), and 75.1⁰ (215) [55–57]. Additionally, there are faint peaks at 27.4⁰ (110), 36.13⁰ (101), 41.3⁰ (111), and 56.7⁰ (220), which correspond to the diffraction pattern of the rutile TiO2 phase [58,59]. The phase percentage formed was determined from the integrated intensity at 2θ= 25.4° for anatase and the peak at 2θ= 27.5° for rutile. The anatase phase percentage (A %) calculation is given in Eq. 2, where IR and IA are the intensities of rutile and anatase peaks [60]. Table S1 presents the results.
(2)
A ,

1 + 1.265 (
I

/
I
A
)

According to the A% results, it is evident that both TiO2 (89.39 %) and MCQD/TiO2 (86.41 %) predominantly exhibit the anatase phase. Notably, the Exfol.CQD/TiO2 structure demonstrates 100 % anatase composition, further emphasizing its potential for superior photocatalytic activity under UV light compared to alternative crystal structures like rutile or brookite [61].

When comparing Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD/TiO2 with pristine TiO2, the XRD patterns of the composites exhibit a similar pattern. There were no characteristic diffraction peaks of carbon in the Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD/TiO2 composites because of the low content of CQDs [53,62]. The Debye-Sherrer (Eq. (3)) was employed to calculate the crystalline size of TiO2, Exfol.CQD/TiO2, and MCQD/TiO2, and the results are presented in Table S1.
(3)

k λ
β cos θ
Where k is Sherrer constant (
k = 0.9 )
, λ is the wavelength of the x-ray beam (Cu Kα, with λ = 0.1540598 nm), β (in rad) is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the intense peak (25.3⁰) and θ (in rad) is the Braggs angle.

Results show that the FWHM diffraction peak at 25.3 ⁰ has been wider, and a slight reduction was observed in the crystalline size of Exfol.CQD/TiO2 (24.83 nm) and MCQD/TiO2 (24.29 nm), as compared to the pristine TiO2 (26.78 nm). The increase in FWHM and reduction in crystal size indicate the incorporation of Exfol.CQD and MCQD in the composites have hindered the growth of TiO2 crystals. [63,64].

XPS analyses were conducted to elucidate the structural characteristics of synthesized pristine MCQD, Exfol.CQD, TiO2, and Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD/TiO2 composites. The XPS full scan spectra ( Fig. 3(a)) for Exfol.CQD and MCQD exhibited two prominent peaks corresponding to carbon (C 1 s, 285.11 eV) and oxygen (O 1 s, 531.36 eV), with a smaller ratio of nitrogen (N 1 s, 399.88 eV) for MCQD [65–67]. The high-resolution spectra of O 1 s (Fig. 3(b)) reveal the presence of C-OH and CO functional groups at binding energies around 531.67 eV and 531.06 eV (MCQD) and 531.48 eV, 532.57 eV (Exfol.CQD) [65,68,69]. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 3(c)) outlined CC/C-C (284.37 eV), C-O/C-OH/C-N (285.02 eV), and CO (287.84 eV) groups [65,68,70] in both CQDs as well. The N 1 s spectra displayed two weak peaks around 399.52 and 401.08 eV, confirming the presence of C-N and N-H/CN in MCQD [70].

In the XPS survey spectrum of pure TiO2, Exfol.CQD and MCQD-loaded photocatalytic composites (Fig. 3(a)), major peaks for Ti 2p, O 1 s, and C 1 s were observed around 459, 530, and 285 eV [71,72]. The high-resolution Ti 2p spectra given in Fig. 3(d) displayed peaks at 464.1 and 458.3 eV (TiO2), 464.3 and 458.5 eV (Exfol.CQD/TiO2), and 464.8 and 459 eV (MCQD/TiO2), corresponding to the binding energies of Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 which is characteristic of Ti4+, with a peak separation of 5.8 eV. This aligns with XRD results, validating the formation of the tetragonal anatase phase TiO2 [56,73,74]. The red shift in these peaks in composites suggests the interaction between CQDs and TiO2 [75,76].

Deconvolution of the high-resolution O 1 s spectra (Fig. 3(b)) revealed peaks at binding energies of 531.3 and 529.64 eV in TiO2 corresponding to C-OH and Ti-O groups. Similar functional groups were observed in Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD/TiO2, with redshifts to higher wavelengths at 531.61, 529.74 eV, and 532, 530.31 eV. These shifts in the binding energies suggest the change in the chemical state of Ti and the formation of the Ti-O-C bond between CQDs and TiO2 [17,47,49,67,68], which agrees with the FTIR results. In C 1 s spectrum of composites given in Fig. 3(c), peaks at 288.46, 286.89, and 284.72 eV (Exfol.CQD/TiO2), and 288.78, 287.36, and 285.43 eV (MCQD/TiO2) are due to OC-O, C-O, and C-C/CC bonds supporting the presence of MCQD and Exfol.CQD in the composites [58,75,76]. When comparing the C1s spectra of composites with pure CQDs, shifts to higher binding energies and changes in peak intensity are evident, reflecting a stronger coupling effect [77,78].

An extensive analysis of the morphology, size distribution, and structure of the MCQDs and Exfol.CQDs were conducted and elucidated through TEM, as depicted in Fig. 4(a) and (c). This was complemented by a size distribution histogram presented in Fig. 4(b) and (d). The MCQDs and Exfol.CQDs exhibited a predominantly spherical shape, and their average diameter, determined through Gaussian fitting curve analysis (given in Table S2-S3), was found to be 2.88 nm and 2.76 nm, as indicated in the offset of Fig. 4(b)-(d). Fig. 4(e)-(f) displays Aberration Corrected HRTEM images of MCQD/TiO2, Exfol.CQD/TiO2 composites featuring lattice spacings of 0.25 nm and 0.36 nm. The 0.25 nm lattice spacing is associated with CQDs, resembling the (100) plane lattice spacing of sp2 graphite carbon. Meanwhile, the 0.36 nm spacing is linked to the (101) plane of TiO2, which is also seen in HRTEM images of pure TiO2 (Fig. 4(g)). MCQD and Exfol.CQD were synthesized in two different methods of hydrothermal and electrochemical exfoliation. The literature on the synthesis of CQDs indicates that the properties of CQDs, including their lattice spacing, can be influenced by different synthesis methods [79]. However, the specific impact of synthesis methods on the lattice spacing of CQDs is not detailed. In this study, no variation was observed in the lattice spacing of MCQD/TiO2 and Exfol.CQD/TiO2 composites between two methods of CQD growth. The consistent lattice spacings suggest a high degree of structural uniformity and consistency in the composites, regardless of the synthesis method used for CQD fabrication, while maintaining the same composites' synthesis method. The stability in lattice spacings across different synthesis methods may suggest that the CQDs within the composites are stable and retain their structural integrity. Consistent lattice spacings are likely to result in predictable electronic, optical, and mechanical properties, which is beneficial for applications that require uniformity in material properties.

To examine the optical properties of Exfol.CQDs and MCQDs, UV-Vis absorption, and emission spectra were measured at room temperature. In the UV-Vis spectrum (Fig. S2), the absorption peaks around 275 nm, and the shoulder bands at ca.∼220MCQD, ∼230Exfol.CQD and ca. 300–400 nm are attributed to the π–π* transition of the CC bond and n-π* transition of the CO and CN bonds [80]. The photographs of synthesized Exfol.CQD and MCQDs were given under daylight and 365 nm UV light (see Fig. S2 inset). The obtained CQDs are brown and yellow under daylight irradiation but green and blue under a 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, signifying CQDs' relatively high quantum yield and offering additional evidence of their successful formation [50,81].

PL measurements were performed to examine the optical properties of CQDs. Fig. S3 (a)-(b) displays the PL spectra of MCQD and Exfol.CQD in ultra-pure water at a concentration of 0.1 mg/ml. The spectra were measured under different excitation wavelengths, ranging from 340 nm to 500 nm at 10 nm intervals. The results for MCQDs indicate that the PL intensity increased, and the emission wavelength position red-shifted as the excitation wavelength raised from 330 to 370 nm. At an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, the highest PL intensity was observed at the emission wavelength of 450 nm. However, the PL intensity decreased at higher excitation wavelengths within the 380–500 nm range, and the emission wavelength position red-shifted. While in Exfol.CQD (Fig. S3 (b)), results indicate that the PL intensity decreases and redshifts as the excitation wavelength rises from 340 to 500 nm, with the highest emission peak at 450 nm. Results showed that surface-trapped electrons or excitons in Exfol.CQDs and MCQDs exhibited excitation-dependent PL behavior due to diverse particle sizes, trapping, and radiative recombination with oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon-containing functional groups [82–84].

PL study was also carried out to study the charge separation efficiency of pure TiO2, Exfol.CQD and MCQD decorated photocatalytic composites under 380 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. S4). In general, lower PL spectra intensity corresponds to a lower recombination rate of electron-hole and effective promotion of the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, which is favorable in photocatalytic reactions [15,64]. It is evident that pure TiO2 showed the strongest luminescence. In Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD/ TiO2 composites, the intensity of PL spectra decreased while the PL intensity of MCQD/TiO2 was much lower. The figure showing the PL intensity of MCQD/TiO2 was given as an inset to see the result more clearly. Results showed that the addition of Exfol.CQDs and MCQDs to pure TiO2 caused a decrease in PL intensity. It could be said that incorporating CQDs into TiO2 reduced the photo-generated electron-hole pairs recombination possibility and thus may enhance the photocatalytic activity [15,64]. This behavior aligns with XPS results, which revealed shifts in binding energies and the formation of Ti-O-C bonds in the CQD/TiO₂ composites. These interactions between CQDs and TiO₂ enhance charge separation and reduce recombination rates, improving photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis DRS analysis of prepared catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: To precisely evaluate the photophysical and photochemical properties of TiO2 and CQD-doped TiO2 semiconductors, UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) analysis was conducted, as shown in Fig. 5(a)-(b). From Fig. 5(a), TiO2 exhibited a reflectance of approximately 55 % above the 400 nm wavelength, while the CQD-doped TiO2 photocatalysts demonstrated a significant reduction in reflectance within the 425–800 nm range. Notably, Exfol.CQD/TiO2 showed a considerably lower reflectance (∼37 %) than MCQD/TiO2 (∼47 %), indicating enhanced visible light absorption consistent with optical analysis results [85].

The bandgap energy of a semiconductor, representing the energy required to excite an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, was calculated for TiO2, MCQD/TiO2, and Exfol.CQD/TiO2 photocatalysts using the Kubelka-Munk model (Eq. (4)) [86].

vs. hv . Where n = 2 (an indirect band gap semiconductor) is depicted in Fig. 5(b). The region showing a steep, linear increase of light absorption with increasing energy is characteristic of semiconductor materials. The x-axis intersection point of the linear fit of the Tauc plot gives an estimate of the band gap energy [85]. For TiO2, MCQD/TiO2 and Exfol.CQD/TiO2, the band gap was found at 3.04, 2.95, and 2.80 eV, respectively. The reduction in the bandgap upon introducing CQDs onto the surface of TiO2, particularly more pronounced in Exfol.CQD/TiO2, indicates enhanced photon absorption in the visible region. This leads to increased generation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, thereby significantly improving the photocatalytic efficiency of the composite photocatalysts [87].

Wang et al. conducted a study examining the decoration of CQDs on TiO2 nanorods (NRs). They reported optical band gaps of 2.94 eV for TiO2 NRs and 2.93 eV for CQD-decorated TiO2 NRs, indicating almost negligible reduction relative to our findings. A more significant reduction in the band gap was observed only when gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) were incorporated into the CQD-TiO2 framework, resulting in a band gap of 2.73 eV [88]. This reduction in band gap values is attributed to the incorporation of gold nanoparticles. The observed disparity in results may be attributed to the differing synthesis methodologies employed. In this case, the hydrothermal approach utilizing citric acid monohydrate and tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane for the preparation of CQDs contrasts with the biomass-derived CQDs that we utilized.

EIS analyses were employed to investigate the charge transfer properties and the role of Exfol.CQD, MCQDs in the interface charge transfer process of TiO2. The Nyquist plots for the pristine Exfol.CQD, MCQD, TiO2, Exfol.CQD/TiO2, and MCQD/TiO2 composites are presented in Fig. 6(a). The inset of Fig. 6(a) illustrates the equivalent circuit model used to fit the EIS data. Table S4 provides the fitting results for charge transfer resistance (Rct) and ohmic series resistance (Rs). The semi-circular arc in the Nyquist plot reveals the Rct at the heterojunction interface, while Rs reflects electrolyte solution resistance and the resistance of the coated FTO substrate [89,90]. The introduction of CQDs in Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD-TiO2 composites reduced the radius of the Nyquist plot in the following order: TiO2 > MCQD/TiO2 > Exfol.CQD/TiO2. As seen in Fig. 6(a), both composites, namely Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD/TiO2 responded with smaller semi-circles compared to pristine TiO2, where Rct values were 252.33 Ω, 193.47 Ω, and 428.33 Ω for Exfol.CQD/TiO2, MCQD/TiO2, and TiO2, respectively. The smaller semi-circle radius indicates a decrease in the interface layer resistance, which leads to the higher efficiency of charge transfer, a decrease in intragranular resistance, which results in the inhibition of fast recombination between charge carriers, effective separation of electron-hole pairs, and enhancement of photocatalytic activity by the introduction of CQDs [57,91]. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that MCQD exhibits the lowest Rct [57,91]. Also, Rs values reveal significant differences in the intrinsic resistance of the materials. The high Rₛ value of MCQD (114.53 Ω) indicates a relatively poor conductive pathway, which may hinder electron transport. Meanwhile, the low Rₛ of Exfol.CQD (30.03 Ω) suggests superior conductivity, probably due to its unique synthesis method, resulting in fewer defects and better electron mobility [92,93]. This finding is consistent with the FTIR results, where MCQDs exhibit additional peaks indicating C-N and C-O-C bonds. These bonds suggest a more complex surface chemistry with possible amine and ether groups, which could contribute to higher resistance and poorer electron transport properties compared to Exfol.CQDs. The MCQD/TiO₂ and Exfol.CQD/TiO₂ exhibit reduced Rₛ values (36.33 and 37.62 Ω) compared to pure TiO₂ (53.76 Ω), indicating that incorporating CQDs improves the conductivity of the TiO₂ matrix. This improved conductivity enhances the photocatalytic activities of the composites. The lower Rs values suggest that the CQDs facilitate better electron transport within the TiO₂ matrix, reducing the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. This efficient charge separation and enhanced electron mobility directly contribute to the improved photocatalytic performance of the composites. Therefore, the incorporation of CQDs not only modifies the surface chemistry but also significantly enhances the electrical properties, leading to more effective photocatalytic degradation processes.

Chronoamperometry measurements were conducted to evaluate the transient photocurrent response for synthesized photocatalysts. Fig. 6(b) shows the transient photocurrent response under blue light irradiation for pristine TiO2, Exfol.CQDs, MCQDs, and composite photocatalysts. The photocurrent response increased immediately upon light activation and then gradually decreased to zero upon deactivation of the incident light, indicating the predominant transport of photo-generated electrons [94]. The Exfol.CQD and MCQD, derived from graphite rod and mussel shell biomass source, exhibited predominant transport of photo-generated electrons. The photocurrent intensity of Exfol.CQD/TiO2 was found to be higher than that of MCQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts, suggesting a more effective separation efficiency of holes and electrons and a higher quantity of photoinduced carrier generation [15,24] confirming EIS results. Also, the photocurrent density of both composites surpassed that of bare TiO2. This enhanced performance could be attributed to the synergistic photocatalytic behavior, which facilitates faster interfacial charge transfer to the electron acceptor in the Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD/TiO2 composites, resulting in a more effective separation of electron-hole pairs [15,23,95]. Additionally, the high surface area, small size, and presence of a negative charge density on the CQD surface may also be responsible for this catalytic activity [96].

The charge transfer dynamics of photocatalysts were studied using the IMPS/IMVS techniques. These techniques are an effective method to investigate the transport dynamics of photogenerated carriers. The electron transport time (Ƭt) and electron recombination time (ƬR) are calculated by the following equations, respectively:

Where ƒIMPS and ƒIMVS are the frequency at the imaginary minimum in the IMPS and IMVS Nyquist plots, respectively. The electron transport time (Ƭt) represents the average time it takes for photogenerated electrons to transfer from the film to the FTO conductive substrate, while the electron recombination time (ƬR) represents the lifetime of the majority of carriers under open circuit conditions. The IMPS and IMVS Nyquist plots of bare TiO2, Exfol.CQD and MCQD decorated TiO2 nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 6(c)-(d). As shown, the Ƭt decreases in the order of TiO2 > MCQD/TiO2 > Exfol.CQD/TiO2 (Fig. 6(d) and Table S5) suggest that the composites' electrons transfer more quickly than pure TiO2. Furthermore, the ƬR increases in the order: Exfol.CQD/TiO2 > MCQD/TiO2 > TiO2, suggesting the lowest electron recombination rate in Exfol.CQD/TiO2 (Table S5) and the fastest electron recombination time in bare TiO2. In the case of TiO2 and composites, it was found that both composites demonstrated a shorter Ƭt compared to TiO2, which exhibited the fastest electron transport in Exfol.CQD/ TiO2 and MCQDs/TiO2. Furthermore, the composites demonstrated a longer ƬR compared to bare TiO2. Notably, nanocomposites showed improved Ƭt and ƬR properties compared to TiO2, indicating that electron recombination in TiO2 photocatalyst is efficiently suppressed by creating oxygen vacancy (OV) and decorating OV-rich CQDs. These results were consistent with the photocurrent results. Electron collection efficiency (ηc) is calculated using Eq. (7) to further study the competition between electron transfer and recombination. The ηc values of MCQDs/TiO2 and Exfol.CQD/ TiO2 were found to be 96 % and 99.2 %, respectively, which was higher than that of TiO2 (84.1 %) (see Table S5). The high ηc values of MCQDs/TiO₂ and Exfol.CQD/TiO₂ indicate that the incorporation of CQDs significantly enhances electron transport and reduces recombination, leading to more efficient and effective photocatalytic degradation processes.

The photodegradation of PFOA over TiO2, Exfol.CQD/TiO2, and MCQD/TiO2 photocatalysts under UVC (200–280 nm wavelength) and visible light irradiation (> 400 nm wavelength) are given in Fig. 7(a)-(b). Results revealed that over pure TiO2, the degradation of PFOA was 41 % under UVC irradiation and 24 % under visible light irradiation. However, upon incorporating Exfol.CQDs into TiO2 (Exfol.CQD/TiO2), the degradation percentages notably increased to 69 % and 50 % under UVC and visible light irradiation, respectively, conforming to the IMVS/IMPS results. The limitations of pure TiO2 lead to inefficient electron-hole pair formation and ineffective utilization of reactive oxygen species, thus limiting its photocatalytic activity. However, the introduction of Exfol.CQDs enhances the performance of TiO₂ by promoting electron transfer within the valence and conduction bands of the Exfol.CQDs/TiO₂ composites. This integration effectively addresses the limitations of bare TiO₂ in electron-hole pair generation. Exfoliated CQDs serve as electron traps, thus impeding the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Consequently, this process enhances the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO₂ for PFOA photodegradation [4,31]. Fig. 8 and Eq. (8) – (16) present the proposed mechanism and pathway of PFOA decomposition using TiO₂ and CQD/TiO₂ photocatalysts. When the TiO2 or CQD/TiO2 photocatalysts absorb a һν with energy equal or greater than band gap energy, e-- h+ pair will separate into a free e- and a free h+ (Eq. 8). Different mechanisms may be involved in the decomposition of PFCA [28,31] under this situation. The h+ in the valence band (VB) has a high oxidation capacity, which can degrade PFOA directly. This step involves the direct e- transfer from the dissociated PFCA anion to the valence band of photocatalyst, generating a perfluoroperoxyl radical

In the case of MCQD/TiO2 composite, the photodegradation of PFOA decreased unexpectedly to 29 % and 20 % under UVC and visible light irradiation, respectively. The potential cooperative effects of MCQD in TiO2 in augmenting photocatalytic activity may not have been realized as expected. This result may be explained by the non-uniform distribution of particles in the structure as seen from TEM images of MCQD, (see Fig. 4(b)). As seen in Fig. 4, although the average particle size of MCQD (2.88 nm) and Exfol.CQD (2.76 nm) are similar; there are agglomerated parts in MCQD that can hinder the photocatalytic activity of MCQD-loaded TiO2 sample [97,98]. Following TEM results, PL analysis showed the excitation and emission wavelengths of MCQD (370 nm and 450 nm) and Exfol.CQD (340 nm and 450 nm) are very close. As known well, the particles with similar sizes get excited at similar wavelengths, and CQDs with the smaller size get excited at a lower wavelength, however, larger-sized CQDs get excited at a higher wavelength [99].

In the comparison of photocatalytic activities of Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD/TiO2, Fig. 6(b) illustrates the photocurrent density of Exfol.CQD/TiO2 was significantly higher than that of MCQD/TiO2. This suggests that Exfol.CQD/TiO2 possesses more effective photogenerated carriers due to the increased light harvesting ability and charge transfer efficiency [15]. This result was confirmed with IMVS/IMPS results and a higher band gap of MCQD/TiO2 (see Fig. 5(b) and Table S5). The electron transfer time (Ƭt) of Exfol.CQD/TiO2 (0.3172 ms) is much faster than that of MCQD/TiO2 (1.0033 ms), which is favorable for photocatalytic efficiency. Similarly, the electron recombination time (TR) of Exfol.CQD/TiO2 (35.9 ms) is longer than MCQD/TiO2 (25.2 ms), that indicates the valence band holes (h+), which are effective in PFOA photocatalytic degradation (Eq. 9), remain on the catalyst surface for a longer time. In addition, the specific interaction between MCQDs and PFOA might differ from Exfol.CQDs. The surface chemistry of MCQDs, including the presence of C-N and C-O-C bonds, might not facilitate the optimal adsorption and subsequent degradation of PFOA as effectively as Exfol.CQDs. These additional functional groups could potentially introduce sites for non-radiative recombination, reducing the overall photocatalytic efficiency. These findings highlight the potential for further research and optimization needed to synthesize MCQD using the hydrothermal method. By fine-tuning parameters such as reaction time, the concentration of mussel shell powder, and applied temperature, it is possible to achieve more homogeneous and effective CQDs. Such enhancements are likely to improve the photocatalytic performance of MCQD/TiO₂ composites significantly. Continued research in this area promises to unlock greater efficiencies and broader applications for these innovative materials.

The photodegradation of short-chain PFCAs, including PFHxA-C6, PFPeA-C5, PFBA-C4, and PFPrA-C3, under visible light irradiation, was further investigated using an Exfol.CQD/TiO2 photocatalyst composite ( Fig. 9). The study achieved degradation rates of 40 % PFHxA-C6, 35 % PFPeA-C5, 31.6 % PFBA-C4 and 21.6 % PFPrA-C3. These results indicate that degradation efficiency decreases with shorter chain lengths since shorter-chain PFCAs are more resistant to further degradation and require higher reaction energy [7,14,100]. However, these findings are promising since short-chain PFCAs are typically resistant to degradation methods such as activated sludge, anaerobic digestion, and trickling filtration. Compared to existing literature, Hori et al. [101] reported degradation rates of 16.3 % for PFBA-C4 and 24.3 % for PFPeA-C5 using direct photolysis in the presence of Fe3+ ions as photocatalysts, while Liu et al. [100] noted that PFCAs with chain lengths of C1–C3 are barely degradable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of intermediates and defluorination results
ce:section.ce:para: The concentrations of short-chain intermediates formed during PFOA photodegradation using Exfol.CQD/TiO2 photocatalysts under visible and UVC light irradiation are presented in Fig. 10 (a)–(b). Under visible light irradiation (Fig. 10 (a)), intermediates with longer carbon chains exhibited peak concentrations during the process. The formation of PFHxA-C6 and PFPeA-C5 increased up to 420 min. (9.8 µg/L) and 240 min. (8.2 µg/L), respectively. In contrast, PFBA-C4 and PFPrA-C3 continued to increase throughout the process, with noticeable formation observed after 180 and 240 minutes, respectively. This trend is attributed to the higher bond strength of the C–F bonds in shorter-chain PFCAs. These observations indicate that as longer-chain intermediates (C5–C6) were generated during PFOA photodegradation, they underwent further degradation, contributing to the formation of shorter-chain PFCAs (C3–C4) [102]. Under UVC light irradiation, the formation rates and concentrations of intermediates (Fig. 10 (b)) were higher compared to visible light irradiation, attributed to the higher energy of the shorter wavelength. The formation of PFHxA-C6 reached 11.5 µg/L, and PFPeA-C5 peaked at 11.4 µg/L at 360 minutes of process time, with PFPeA-C5 further increasing to 11.9 µg/L by the end of the photodegradation process. Meanwhile, the formation of PFBA-C4 and PFPrA-C3 showed a delayed increase but continued to rise until the end of the reaction. This behavior again suggests that the degradation of longer-chain PFCAs (C5–C6) contributed to the formation of shorter-chain PFCAs (C3–C4). Notably, after the photodegradation process (Fig. 10(b)), the intermediate concentrations followed the order: PFBA-C4 (13.6 µg/L) > PFPeA-C5 (11.9 µg/L) > PFPrA-C3 (9.4 µg/L) > PFHxA-C6 (6.7 µg/L). The findings suggest that the intermediates formed during the initial stages were primarily comprised of longer-chain species (C5-C6). Subsequently, these intermediates underwent a stepwise decomposition involving the sequential removal of CF₂ units. This decomposition pathway illustrates the gradual breakdown of PFOA molecules, emphasizing the dynamic nature of the intermediates formed during the process. As the CF₂ units are eliminated, the structural configuration of the intermediates evolves, resulting in the formation of shorter chains.

The defluorination results of PFOA using TiO₂ and Exfol.CQD/TiO₂ photocatalysts under visible and UVC light irradiation, along with the short-chain PFCAs (C3–C6) (under visible light irradiation) at the end of the photodegradation process, are presented in Fig. 11. The defluorination efficiency of 100 µg/L PFOA significantly increased with the incorporation of Exfol.CQDs onto the TiO₂ surface compared to pure TiO₂ photocatalysts. Under visible light irradiation, the defluorination percentage improved from 11.6 % (TiO₂) to 32 % (Exfol.CQD/TiO₂), with fluoride releases increasing from 8 µg/L to 22 µg/L. Similarly, under UVC irradiation, defluorination efficiency rose from 21.8 % (TiO₂) to 34.5 % (Exfol.CQD/TiO₂), with fluoride release increasing from 15 µg/L to 23.7 µg/L. Additionally, the fluoride release in photodegradation of the short-chain PFCAs (C3–C6) mixture increased from 8 µg/L (3.42 %) to 13 µg/L (5.57 %), further highlighting the enhanced degradation efficiency of the Exfol.CQD/TiO₂ photocatalyst composites.

The results demonstrate that the introduction of Exfol.CQDs onto the surface of TiO₂ significantly enhance the photocatalytic degradation and defluorination efficiency of PFOA and its intermediates under both visible and UVC light irradiation. This improvement is attributed to the improved light absorption, charge separation, and catalytic activity provided by the CQDs, making CQD/TiO₂ photocatalyst composites a promising approach for addressing PFAS contamination in environmental remediation.

To evaluate the reusability and stability of MCQD/TiO2 and Exfol.CQD/TiO2 photocatalyst composites, we conducted three-cycle experiments focused on the photodegradation of PFOA under visible light irradiation. After each cycle, filtration paper separated the photocatalyst composites from the PFOA solution. The composites were then washed three times with ultrapure water and ethanol. After drying in an oven at 65°C for 6 hours, they were reused to degrade a fresh PFOA solution. The results presented in Fig. 12 demonstrated the successful reusability of both MCQD/TiO2 and Exfol.CQD/TiO2 photocatalysts, as their degradation rates remained consistent after three cycles of use. This indicates that both composites maintained their stability throughout the experiments. These findings underscore the potential of MCQD/TiO2 and Exfol.CQD/TiO2 photocatalysts for repeated use in wastewater treatment applications, particularly for effectively degrading PFCA compounds. Their robustness and sustained performance make them promising candidates for practical implementation in environmental remediation efforts.

This study represents a pioneering investigation into the photodegradation of PFOA using CQD/TiO2 composites under both visible and UVC light for PFCA remediation applications. The successful synthesis of CQDs through electrochemical exfoliation and hydrothermal methods, as well as the synthesis of Exfol.CQD/TiO2 and MCQD/TiO2 photocatalyst composites using the sol-gel method were confirmed through comprehensive structural and morphological analyses using FTIR, XPS, XRD, TEM, and HRTEM techniques. Detailed insights into the enhanced optical and electrochemical properties of the composites were provided by UV-Vis, PL spectroscopy, EIS, photocurrent measurements, IMVS/IMPS, and photocatalysts’ band gap energies were estimated using UV-Vis-DRS. Photodegradation experiments revealed a significant enhancement in PFOA degradation over Exfol.CQD/TiO2 composites compared to pure TiO2. Under UVC and visible light irradiation, Exfol.CQD/TiO2 achieved degradation efficiencies of 69 % and 50 %, respectively, compared to 41 % and 24 % with pure TiO2. This improvement is attributed to the incorporation of Exfol.CQDs, which enhances the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 by facilitating electron transfer within the valence and conduction bands of the composites, thereby overcoming the limitations posed by the large bandgap of pure TiO2. In contrast, the MCQD/TiO2 composites exhibited lower photodegradation efficiencies of 29 % and 20 % under UVC and visible light, respectively. This unexpected result may be due to the non-uniform distribution of particles in the structure of MCQD. Following the photodegradation results of PFOA, the photodegradation of short-chain PFCAs (C3-C6) using an Exfol.CQD/TiO2 photocatalyst under visible light irradiation was conducted. The study demonstrated promising degradation efficiencies: 40 % for PFHxA-C6, 35 % for PFPeA -C5, 31.6 % for PFBA-C4, and 21.6 % for PFPrA-C3. These results are encouraging, as short-chain PFCAs are typically resistant to degradation. Also, the defluorination efficiency of PFOA and its short-chain intermediates using Exfol.CQD/TiO₂ photocatalysts under visible and UVC light irradiation were evaluated, along with the fluoride release and defluorination ratios following the photodegradation of short-chain PFCAs (C3–C6). The incorporation of Exfol.CQDs significantly enhanced light absorption, charge separation, and overall catalytic activity, resulting in higher defluorination percentages and fluoride release compared to pure TiO₂. Moreover, the stepwise decomposition of PFOA into longer-chain intermediates (C5–C6) followed by shorter-chain intermediates (C3–C4) has been effectively demonstrated, with significant enhancements in degradation rates observed under UVC light irradiation.

Overall, this study unveils the transformative potential of Exfol.CQD/TiO2 for the enhanced photodegradation of persistent organic pollutants like PFOA and short-chain PFCAs. By emphasizing the critical role of nanoparticle size and integration methods, our findings illuminate new pathways for future research. These advancements promise to optimize photocatalytic systems, not only for environmental remediation but also for a wide array of innovative applications. This breakthrough sets the stage for the next generation of sustainable solutions in combating environmental pollution.
